bibliographic_data.title: Certified Side Channels
bibliographic_data.abstract: We demonstrate that the format in which private keys are persisted impacts Side Channel Analysis (SCA) security. Surveying several widely deployed software libraries, we investigate the formats they support, how they parse these keys, and what runtime decisions they make. We uncover a combination of weaknesses and vulnerabilities, in extreme cases inducing completely disjoint multi-precision arithmetic stacks deep within the cryptosystem level for keys that otherwise seem logically equivalent. Exploiting these vulnerabilities, we design and implement key recovery attacks utilizing signals ranging from electromagnetic (EM) emanations, to granular microarchitecture cache timings, to coarse traditional wall clock timings.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper demonstrates that the format in which private keys are persisted can significantly impact SCA security by inducing different behaviors and vulnerabilities within software libraries.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors surveyed several widely deployed software libraries to investigate key formats, parsing methods, and runtime decisions. They uncovered weaknesses and vulnerabilities leading to different arithmetic stacks for logically equivalent keys.
outcomes: Identified vulnerabilities in key persistence formats
Designed and implemented key recovery attacks using various side channels
core_content.full_text_markdown: The paper challenges the assumption that higher-level protocols or systems built upon cryptographic primitives will naturally benefit from SCA mitigations. It demonstrates that the format of private keys can significantly impact real-world SCA security, leading to different behaviors within software libraries and vulnerabilities in key parsing methods. The authors provide detailed recommendations for secure key persistence and highlight the importance of considering these factors in cryptographic implementations.
